devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । अथ ह तमुत्पत्त्यैवाभिव्यज्यमानभगवल्लक्षणं साम्योपशमवैराग्यैश्वर्यमहा विभूतिभिरनुदिनमेधमानानुभावं प्रकृतयः प्रजा ब्राह्मणा ...
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvāca atha ha tamutpattyaivābhivyajyamānabhagavallakṣaṇaṃ sāmyopaśamavairāgyaiśvaryamahā vibhūtibhiranudinamedhamānānubhāvaṃ prakṛtayaḥ prajā brāhmaṇā devatāścāvanitalasamavanāyātitarāṃ jagṛdhuḥ

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
It is reported that after the incarnation, Lord Ṛṣabha had all the characteristic marks of the Supreme Lord (such as the marks of thunderbolt, goad etc.) manifest (on the soles of his feet), from his very birth. His majestic splendour was everyday enhanced by qualities like impartiality and equality towards all, control of internal and external senses, non-attachment to sense-pleasures, domination and control over all beings, and possession of great spiritual powers. The king’s counsellors, subjects, god-like Brāhmaṇas very eagerly wished to have him as the protector of the earth.

devanagari: तस्य ह वा इत्थं वर्ष्मणा वरीयसा बृहच्छ्लोकेन चौजसा बलेन श्रिया यशसा वीर्यशौर्याभ्यां च पिता ऋषभ इतीदं नाम चकार ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya ha vā itthaṃ varṣmaṇā varīyasā bṛhacchlokena caujasā balena śriyā yaśasā vīryaśauryābhyāṃ ca pitā ṛṣabha itīdaṃ nāma cakāra

translation: On account of his Supreme-most personality which was extolled in laudatory verses by poets, his abundant vigour, physical strength, splendour, glory, dominance and valour, his father significantly named him Ṛṣabha (the most excellent).

devanagari: यस्य हीन्द्रः स्पर्धमानो भगवान्वर्षे न ववर्ष तदवधार्य भगवानृषभदेवो योगेश्वरः प्रहस्यात्मयोगमायया स्ववर्षमजनाभं नामाभ्यवर्षत् ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: yasya hīndraḥ spardhamāno bhagavānvarṣe na vavarṣa tadavadhārya bhagavānṛṣabhadevo yogeśvaraḥ prahasyātmayogamāyayā svavarṣamajanābhaṃ nāmābhyavarṣat

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: The mighty Indra became jealous of him, and did not rain in his continent (Ajanābha, later known as Bhārata- varṣa). When apprised of it, Ṛṣabhadeva, the Master of yoga pooh-poohed it with a laughter, and profusely showered rains over his Varṣa (continent) called Ajanābha, by his mystic (yogic) power.

devanagari: नाभिस्तु यथाभिलषितं सुप्रजस्त्वमवरुध्यातिप्रमोदभरविह्वलो गद्गदाक्षरया गिरा स्वैरं गृहीतनरलोकसधर्मं भगवन्तं पुराणपुरुषं मायाविलसितमतिर्वत्...
english_devnagari: nābhistu yathābhilaṣitaṃ suprajastvamavarudhyātipramodabharavihvalo gadgadākṣarayā girā svairaṃ gṛhītanaralokasadharmaṃ bhagavantaṃ purāṇapuruṣaṃ māyāvilasitamatirvatsa tāteti sānurāgamupalālayanparāṃ nirvṛtimupagataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [vocative single from √ las class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ las class 10 verb]
[vocative single from √ las class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ las class 10 verb]

translation: Being blessed with a worthy child as desired by him, Nābhi was transported with rapturous joy, and affectionately fondled the glorious Lord, the most ancient Person who, of his own free will had assumed a human form. His (Nābhi’s) mind was overpowered with Māyā. He fondly caressed him with faltering words choked with emotion: ‘Oh child, my daddie’ and felt exceedingly happy.

devanagari: विदितानुरागमापौरप्रकृति जनपदो राजा नाभिरात्मजं समयसेतुरक्षायामभिषिच्य ब्राह्मणेषूपनिधाय सह मेरुदेव्या विशालायां प्रसन्ननिपुणेन तपसा समाधि...
english_devnagari: viditānurāgamāpauraprakṛti janapado rājā nābhirātmajaṃ samayaseturakṣāyāmabhiṣicya brāhmaṇeṣūpanidhāya saha merudevyā viśālāyāṃ prasannanipuṇena tapasā samādhiyogena naranārāyaṇākhyaṃ bhagavantaṃ vāsudevamupāsīnaḥ kālena tanmahimānamavāpa
translation:
King Nābhi who regarded the mandate of the public as authoritative, came to know the love and loyalty of all, from common man to the minister (commanded by Ṛṣabha), installed his son for the protection and preservation of the boundaries (standards) of religious conduct. Having entrusted him to the care of Brāhmaṇas[1] he, accompanied by Merudevī, retreated to Viśālā (Badarikāśrama), where he propitiated the venerable Lord Vāsudeva (alternatively) known as Nara and Nārāyaṇa, through severe penance which caused no trouble to others, and through the yoga of profound meditation. In due course, he attained to his greatness, viz., the state of jīvanmukta (being liberated while alive).
Oh descendant of Pāṇḍavas (king Parīkṣit)! The two following verses are sung in his (Nābhi’s) praise:
purport:
Footnote [1]:
According to Jinasena, Nābhi installed Ṛṣabha as an emperor when he was grownup and married. MPS (Tri-ṣaṣṭi-lakṣaṇa Śrī Mahā Purāṇa—Saṅgraha—K.B. Nitwe, Ed. 1899) 16.32.

devanagari: यस्य ह पाण्डवेय श्लोकावुदाहरन्ति । को नु तत्कर्म राजर्षेर्नाभेरन्वाचरेत्पुमान् । अपत्यतामगाद्यस्य हरिः शुद्धेन कर्मणा ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: yasya ha pāṇḍaveya ślokāvudāharanti ko nu tatkarma rājarṣernābheranvācaretpumān apatyatāmagādyasya hariḥ śuddhena karmaṇā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect active third single], [aorist active third single]
[instrumental single from √ śudh class 1 verb], [instrumental single from √ śudh class 4 verb]
[instrumental single from √ śudh class 1 verb], [instrumental single from √ śudh class 4 verb]

translation: What other man can perform the righteous deed as done by the royal sage Nābhi, due to the purity and piety of which act, Hari became his child?

devanagari: ब्रह्मण्योऽन्यः कुतो नाभेर्विप्रा मङ्गलपूजिताः । यस्य बर्हिषि यज्ञेशं दर्शयामासुरोजसा ॥ ७ ॥
translation: Who else than Nābhi could be so devoted to Brāhmaṇas, who, being propitiated by sacrificial fees, by their spiritual power, made the Lord of Sacrifice manifest himself (to him) in his sacrifice?

devanagari: अथ ह भगवानृषभदेवः स्ववर्षं कर्मक्षेत्रमनुमन्यमानः प्रदर्शितगुरुकुलवासो लब्धवरैर्गुरुभिरनुज्ञातो गृहमेधिनां धर्माननुशिक्षमाणो जयन्त्यामिन्...
english_devnagari: atha ha bhagavānṛṣabhadevaḥ svavarṣaṃ karmakṣetramanumanyamānaḥ pradarśitagurukulavāso labdhavarairgurubhiranujñāto gṛhamedhināṃ dharmānanuśikṣamāṇo jayantyāmindradattāyāmubhayalakṣaṇaṃ karma samāmnāyāmnātamabhiyuñjannātmajānāmātmasamānānāṃ śataṃ janayāmāsa

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ yuj class 7 verb], [vocative single from √ yuj class 7 verb]

translation: After his coronation, Lord Ṛṣabhadeva regarded his own continent as Karma-kṣetra[2] (a land fit to perform acts leading to the Heaven and Liberation), and showed (the model of life) how one should stay in the preceptor’s house (for education). He obtained the permission (to enter householder’s life) from his preceptors who were given boons (as fees) of their choice. For teaching the duties of a householder’s life he married Jayantī[3] who was given (in marriage) by Indra. He performed both types of duties as prescribed in the scriptures (of Śrutis and Smṛtis). He begot through her (queen Jayantī) one hundred sons who were like unto him.[4]
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Jinasena interprets karma-bhūmi as a country in which the order of Agriculture and other six ways of livelihood had been fixed by Ṛṣabha (MPS 16.249).

Footnote [3]:
175 According to Jinasena, he married Yaśasvatī and Sunandā with the consent of Indra. These two were the Princesses of Kaccha and Mahākaccha (MPS. 15,69, 70).

Footnote [4]:
Jain tradition confirms the number of sons as 100, but the names are different (MPS. 16.4).

devanagari: येषां खलु महायोगी भरतो ज्येष्ठः श्रेष्ठगुण आसीद्येनेदं वर्षंभारतमिति व्यपदिशन्ति ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: yeṣāṃ khalu mahāyogī bharato jyeṣṭhaḥ śreṣṭhaguṇa āsīdyenedaṃ varṣaṃbhāratamiti vyapadiśanti

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [aorist active third single], [injunctive active third single]

translation: Out of those (sons), the eldest was the great yogi, Bharata, who was endowed with highly excellent attributes. It is on account of him that this continent came to be called Bhārata[5].
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Jain tradition endorses this derivation:
[tam?] nāmnā bhārataṃ varṣam
iti hāsīj janāspadam / MPS. 15.59.

devanagari: तमनु कुशावर्त इलावर्तो ब्रह्मावर्तो मलयः केतुर्भद्रसेन इन्द्रस्पृग्विदर्भः कीकट इति नव नवति प्रधानाः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: tamanu kuśāvarta ilāvarto brahmāvarto malayaḥ keturbhadrasena indraspṛgvidarbhaḥ kīkaṭa iti nava navati pradhānāḥ
translation: After him were born nine sons viz. Kuśāvarta, Ilāvarta, Brahmāvarta, Malaya, Ketu, Bhadrasena, Indraspṛś [Indraspṛg/Indraspṛk], Vidarbha and Kīkaṭa—these were the leaders of (other) ninety (sons).

devanagari: कविर्हविरन्तरिक्षः प्रबुद्धः पिप्पलायनः । आविर्होत्रोऽथ द्रुमिलश्चमसः करभाजनः ॥ ११ ॥
translation: Kavi, Hari, Antarīkṣa, Prabuddha, Pippalāyana, Āvis, Hotra, Drumila, Camasa and Karabhājana—these nine who demonstrated to the world, the sacred duties of votaries of the Lord, were themselves eminent devotees of the glorious Lord. We shall describe to you later on (in Skandha XI) their pious life imbued with the glory of the Lord, and leading a fund of tranquility, in the dialogue of Vasudeva and Nārada.

devanagari: इति भागवतधर्मदर्शना नव महाभागवतास्तेषां सुचरितं भगवन्महिमोपबृंहितं वसुदेवनारदसंवादमुपशमायनमुपरिष्टाद्वर्णयिष्यामः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: iti bhāgavatadharmadarśanā nava mahābhāgavatāsteṣāṃ sucaritaṃ bhagavanmahimopabṛṃhitaṃ vasudevanāradasaṃvādamupaśamāyanamupariṣṭādvarṇayiṣyāmaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ bṛh ], [accusative single from √ bṛh ]
[nominative single from √ bṛh ], [accusative single from √ bṛh ]

translation: Kavi, Hari, Antarīkṣa, Prabuddha, Pippalāyana, Āvis, Hotra, Drumila, Camasa and Karabhājana—these nine who demonstrated to the world, the sacred duties of votaries of the Lord, were themselves eminent devotees of the glorious Lord. We shall describe to you later on (in Skandha XI) their pious life imbued with the glory of the Lord, and leading a fund of tranquility, in the dialogue of Vasudeva and Nārada.

devanagari: यवीयांस एकाशीतिर्जायन्तेयाः पितुरादेशकरा महाशालीना महाश्रोत्रिया यज्ञशीलाः कर्मविशुद्धा ब्राह्मणा बभूवुः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: yavīyāṃsa ekāśītirjāyanteyāḥ piturādeśakarā mahāśālīnā mahāśrotriyā yajñaśīlāḥ karmaviśuddhā brāhmaṇā babhūvuḥ
translation: The eighty-one younger sons of Jayantī who were obedient to their father, and of great modesty, profound scholars of the Vedic lore, habitual performers of sacrifice and of extremely pious deeds, became Brāhmaṇas (i.e. were given to meditation on Brahman).

devanagari: भगवानृषभसंज्ञ आत्मतन्त्रः स्वयं नित्यनिवृत्तानर्थपरम्परः केवलानन्दानुभव ईश्वर एव विपरीतवत्कर्माण्यारभमाणः कालेनानुगतं धर्ममाचरणेनोपशिक्ष...
english_devnagari: bhagavānṛṣabhasaṃjña ātmatantraḥ svayaṃ nityanivṛttānarthaparamparaḥ kevalānandānubhava īśvara eva viparītavatkarmāṇyārabhamāṇaḥ kālenānugataṃ dharmamācaraṇenopaśikṣayannatadvidāṃ sama upaśānto maitraḥ kāruṇiko dharmārthayaśaḥprajānandāmṛtāvarodhena gṛheṣu lokaṃ niyamayat

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ śikṣ ], [vocative single from √ śikṣ ]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: The venerable Lord called Ṛṣabha was completely self-dependent. By his nature, he was eternally free from evils. He enjoyed absolute joy. Though he was the Ruler of the universe (God himself), he performed actions as if he were the opposite (an ordinary human being). He taught the ignorant by his behaviour, the traditional righteous duties. He was equal and impartial, friendly and merciful. He laid down the rules of conduct to people in the householder’s, stage of life, by his achievement of righteousness, wealth, fame, progeny, (earthly) joy and liberation.
Whatever is done by the heads (the leading persons) is followed by the world.[6]
purport:
Footnote [6]:
tad yad ācarati śreṣṭho.... lokastad anuvartate / Bhagavad Gītā 3.21.

devanagari: यद्यपि स्वविदितं सकलधर्मं ब्राह्मं गुह्यं ब्राह्मणैर्दर्शितमार्गेण । सामादिभिरुपायैर्जनतामनुशशास ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: yadyapi svaviditaṃ sakaladharmaṃ brāhmaṃ guhyaṃ brāhmaṇairdarśitamārgeṇa sāmādibhirupāyairjanatāmanuśaśāsa

translation: Even though he knew for himself the confidential, esoteric Vedic lore containing the religious duties of all (people), he governed the people by expedients called sāma, (conciliation) dāna (gift or, bribery), daṇḍa (punishment), bheda (sowing discords) as advised by Brāhmaṇas.

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: As per counsels of Brāhmaṇas, he worshipped the Lord by performing for a hundred times, sacrifices, each provided with all the requisites with reference to the materials, place, time, age (of the sacrificer), faith, (proper) sacrificial priests, and the several gods to be invited.

devanagari: भगवतर्षभेण परिरक्ष्यमाण एतस्मिन्वर्षे न कश्चन पुरुषो । वाञ्छत्यविद्यमानमिवात्मनोऽन्यस्मात्कथञ्चन किमपि कर्हिचिदवेक्षते भर्तर्यनुसवनं । व...
english_devnagari: bhagavatarṣabheṇa parirakṣyamāṇa etasminvarṣe na kaścana puruṣo vāñchatyavidyamānamivātmano vijṛmbhitasnehātiśayamantareṇa

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ vāñch class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ vāñch class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ vāñch class 1 verb], [locative single from √ vāñch class 1 verb]

translation: While Lord Ṛṣabha was protecting this continent (Bhārata-varṣa), no man, at any time, desired or expected anything whatsoever from anybody else, as if it was non-existent (and hence beyond desire or expectation); nor did he cast a wistful glance at another’s possession—except the ever- increasing intense love for their Lord.

devanagari: स कदाचिदटमानो भगवानृषभो ब्रह्मावर्तगतो ब्रह्मर्षिप्रवरसभायां प्रजानां निशामयन्तीनामात्मजानवहितात्मनः प्रश्रयप्रणयभरसुयन्त्रितानप्युपशिक्...
english_devnagari: sa kadācidaṭamāno bhagavānṛṣabho brahmāvartagato brahmarṣipravarasabhāyāṃ prajānāṃ niśāmayantīnāmātmajānavahitātmanaḥ praśrayapraṇayabharasuyantritānapyupaśikṣayanniti hovāca

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Once upon a time, while touring (through his kingdom) Lord Ṛṣabha came to Brahmāvarta.
With a view to admonishing his sons in that conference of great and prominent Brāhmaṇa sages, he spoke thus to his sons even though they had their minds controlled, and their conduct governed by affection and modesty—he did so, within, the sight and hearing of his subjects.
